title: Trishna Saikia
text:
I am a final-year PhD researcher in Computer Science and Engineering at IIT Indore. My research lies at the intersection of machine learning, computer vision, multimodal learning, and physiological computing.
My work focuses on extracting physiological and behavioral information from videos and biosignals for applications in healthcare, including heart rate, blood pressure, respiration, oxygen saturation, and pain analysis.
I am a Prime Minister Research Fellow (PMRF), an India RISE Fellow, and currently a Visiting Research Scholar at the University of Turku, Finland.
Learn more about me →

    
    
  







 Back to top

title: Trishna Saikia
text:
About me

Thanks for stopping by!
I am a final-year PhD researcher in Computer Science and Engineering at the Indian Institute of Technology Indore, working at the intersection of machine learning, computer vision, multimodal learning, and physiological computing.
My research focuses on developing robust AI systems for extracting physiological and behavioral information from videos and biosignals under real-world conditions. I am particularly interested in video-based physiological sensing, healthcare AI, temporal representation learning, multimodal fusion, and trustworthy machine learning.
Get in touch by sending me a note!

title: Trishna Saikia
text:
Research profile


Building AI systems that understand human physiology from videos and biosignals.
During my PhD, I have worked on remote physiological sensing methods for estimating heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, and pain-related responses using face videos, rPPG signals, PPG signals, and multimodal physiological data.
My work includes the design of temporal convolutional networks, transformer-based architectures, cross-modal knowledge distillation frameworks, multimodal fusion strategies, and self-supervised or weakly supervised learning methods for healthcare applications.


Remote physiological sensing from videos and biosignals


Machine learning, computer vision, and multimodal learning


Healthcare AI and physiological computing




My research has led to publications in venues including IEEE TIM, ACM TIST, Digital Signal Processing, Expert Systems with Applications, ICPR, ICMI, and FG. I have also contributed to the development of large-scale physiological video datasets for blood pressure, respiratory rate, and SpO₂ estimation.


PhD in Computer Science and Engineering, IIT Indore, expected 2026


Prime Minister Research Fellow, Government of India


India RISE Fellow, Johns Hopkins University, Gupta-Klinsky India Institute


Visiting Research Scholar, University of Turku, Finland

title: Trishna Saikia
text:
Research interests


Physiological Sensing
rPPG-based estimation of heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation from face videos and biosignals.


Computer Vision
Video-based analysis of physiological and behavioral signals under motion, illumination, compression, and real-world variations.


Multimodal Learning
Fusion of visual, temporal, and physiological modalities for healthcare and affective computing applications.


Healthcare AI
Robust, trustworthy, and deployable AI systems for non-contact health monitoring and biomedical signal analysis.

title: Trishna Saikia
text:
Selected highlights


Publications
14 peer-reviewed publications in journals and conferences.


Datasets
Physiological video datasets covering heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, and SpO₂ estimation.


Fellowship
Prime Minister Research Fellow, India RISE Fellow, and Erasmus+ Visiting Research Scholar.


Mentoring
Experience in teaching, mentoring, and interdisciplinary research collaboration.

title: VOLEMIA: Non-Invasive Blood Pressure Estimation Using Temporal-Spectral Convolutional Network
text: This work proposes VOLEMIA, a non-invasive blood pressure estimation framework based on temporal-spectral learning from PPG signals. The method combines pulse-based decomposition and physiological signal enhancement to improve systolic and diastolic blood pressure estimation.

title: VOLEMIA: Non-Invasive Blood Pressure Estimation Using Temporal-Spectral Convolutional Network
text: This work proposes VOLEMIA, a non-invasive blood pressure estimation framework based on temporal-spectral learning from PPG signals. The method combines pulse-based decomposition and physiological signal enhancement to improve systolic and diastolic blood pressure estimation.

title: VOLEMIA: Non-Invasive Blood Pressure Estimation Using Temporal-Spectral Convolutional Network
text:
Key Contributions

Developed a temporal-spectral convolutional network for SBP and DBP estimation.
Combined temporal waveform information with spectral representations.
Evaluated the method on physiological signal datasets.
Demonstrated promising performance for non-invasive blood pressure monitoring.

title: VOLEMIA: Non-Invasive Blood Pressure Estimation Using Temporal-Spectral Convolutional Network
text:
Publication
Trishna Saikia, Satwik Vankayalapati, Puneet Gupta, and Pasi Liljeberg. “VOLEMIA: Non-Invasive Blood Pressure Estimation Using Temporal-Spectral Convolutional Network.” Digital Signal Processing, 2025.

title: SHINE: Synergizing Transformers with Contrastive Learning for rPPG-Based SpO₂ Estimation
text:
SHINE is a contactless framework for estimating blood oxygen saturation (SpO₂) from facial videos using remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) signals. The work addresses key limitations of existing rPPG-based SpO₂ estimation methods, including their dependence on hand-crafted features, limited ability to model temporal patterns, and weak generalization across diverse skin tones.
The paper was published in Expert Systems with Applications, Volume 296, Part D, 15 January 2026. The DOI is 10.1016/j.eswa.2025.129190.

title: SHINE: Synergizing Transformers with Contrastive Learning for rPPG-Based SpO₂ Estimation
text:
SHINE is a contactless framework for estimating blood oxygen saturation (SpO₂) from facial videos using remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) signals. The work addresses key limitations of existing rPPG-based SpO₂ estimation methods, including their dependence on hand-crafted features, limited ability to model temporal patterns, and weak generalization across diverse skin tones.
The paper was published in Expert Systems with Applications, Volume 296, Part D, 15 January 2026. The DOI is 10.1016/j.eswa.2025.129190.

title: SHINE: Synergizing Transformers with Contrastive Learning for rPPG-Based SpO₂ Estimation
text:
Motivation
SpO₂ is an important physiological indicator for monitoring respiratory and cardiovascular health. Conventional pulse oximeters require direct skin contact, which can be limiting in remote monitoring, neonatal care, infectious disease settings, and other scenarios where non-contact sensing is preferred.
rPPG offers a camera-based alternative by analyzing subtle color variations in facial videos. However, existing rPPG-based SpO₂ systems often rely on traditional feature engineering and may struggle with robustness, especially across different skin tones and real-world recording conditions.

title: SHINE: Synergizing Transformers with Contrastive Learning for rPPG-Based SpO₂ Estimation
text:
Method
SHINE combines three major ideas:

Transformer-inspired temporal modeling The model captures temporal dynamics and global patterns in rPPG-derived signals for improved SpO₂ estimation.
Supervised contrastive learning Contrastive learning is used to strengthen feature representation and improve the generalizability of the system.
Quality-weighted consolidation of RoRs SHINE computes multiple ratio-of-ratios (RoRs) from RGB channel combinations and combines them using a quality-aware strategy, giving more importance to less noisy RoR estimates.

title: SHINE: Synergizing Transformers with Contrastive Learning for rPPG-Based SpO₂ Estimation
text:
Key Contributions

Proposed a transformer-inspired framework for instantaneous and contactless SpO₂ estimation from rPPG signals.
Used supervised contrastive learning to improve RoR feature representation.
Incorporated multiple RGB channel ratio-of-ratios to better handle skin-tone-dependent light absorption differences.
Introduced a quality-weighted consolidation strategy for reliable SpO₂ prediction.
Presented the SpO₂-rPPG dataset, consisting of facial videos with corresponding SpO₂ measurements from subjects with diverse skin tones.
Demonstrated improved performance over existing methods on the proposed dataset and public datasets including PURE and MSPM.

title: SHINE: Synergizing Transformers with Contrastive Learning for rPPG-Based SpO₂ Estimation
text:
Dataset
The work introduces the SpO₂-rPPG dataset, a large-scale facial video dataset for rPPG-based SpO₂ estimation. It contains 305 facial videos recorded for 90 seconds, along with pulse rate and SpO₂ measurements. The dataset includes subjects with diverse skin tones, supporting research on fairness and generalization in contactless physiological sensing.

title: SHINE: Synergizing Transformers with Contrastive Learning for rPPG-Based SpO₂ Estimation
text:
Publication
Vaidehi Agarwal, Trishna Saikia, Anup Kumar Gupta, and Puneet Gupta. “SHINE: Synergizing Transformers with Contrastive Learning for Thriving rPPG-Based SpO₂ Estimation.” Expert Systems with Applications, Volume 296, Part D, 129190, 2026.
DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2025.129190

title: PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals
text:
PainXtract is a multimodal physiological computing framework designed for automatic pain assessment using physiological signals. The system leverages complementary information from multiple biosignals to classify pain into multiple intensity levels, enabling objective and continuous pain monitoring.
The work was presented at the 27th International Conference on Multimodal Interaction (ICMI 2025) and contributes to the growing field of AI-assisted pain assessment and affective computing.

title: PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals
text:
PainXtract is a multimodal physiological computing framework designed for automatic pain assessment using physiological signals. The system leverages complementary information from multiple biosignals to classify pain into multiple intensity levels, enabling objective and continuous pain monitoring.
The work was presented at the 27th International Conference on Multimodal Interaction (ICMI 2025) and contributes to the growing field of AI-assisted pain assessment and affective computing.

title: PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals
text:
Motivation
Pain is one of the most important indicators of a patient’s health status, yet it is often assessed using self-reported scales that can be subjective, inconsistent, or impossible to obtain from certain patient populations such as:

Neonates
Elderly patients
Sedated patients
Individuals with communication impairments

Physiological signals provide an alternative pathway for objective pain assessment. However, individual physiological modalities often capture only part of the body’s response to pain. PainXtract was developed to combine multiple physiological signals and exploit their complementary information for robust pain recognition.

title: PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals
text:
Method
The proposed framework utilizes multiple physiological modalities:

1. Blood Volume Pulse (BVP)
BVP captures cardiovascular responses associated with pain-induced autonomic nervous system activation.


2. Electrodermal Activity (EDA)
EDA measures skin conductance changes caused by sympathetic nervous system responses and is strongly associated with stress and pain.


3. Respiration (RESP)
Respiratory patterns often change under painful conditions and provide valuable information regarding physiological distress.


4. Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂)
SpO₂ signals provide additional physiological context and contribute complementary information to the multimodal system.


5. Multimodal Fusion
PainXtract combines modality-specific information through a multimodal learning framework to improve multiclass pain classification performance compared with individual modalities.

title: PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals
text:
Key Contributions

Proposed a multimodal physiological computing framework for automatic pain assessment.
Developed a multiclass pain classification system capable of distinguishing different pain levels.
Integrated cardiovascular, electrodermal, respiratory, and oxygen saturation signals.
Demonstrated the effectiveness of multimodal fusion over single-modality approaches.
Contributed to the development of objective pain monitoring systems for healthcare applications.

title: PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals
text:
Experimental Evaluation
The framework was evaluated on physiological recordings collected under different pain conditions.
Key findings include:

Multimodal fusion consistently outperformed individual physiological modalities.
Electrodermal Activity (EDA) emerged as one of the most informative pain-related signals.
Combining cardiovascular, respiratory, and electrodermal information improved overall classification performance.
The system demonstrated the potential for real-time and non-invasive pain assessment.

title: PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals
text:
Research Significance
PainXtract extends physiological computing beyond traditional vital-sign estimation and explores the use of physiological signals for affective and behavioral state understanding.
The work contributes toward the development of:

Intelligent healthcare monitoring systems
AI-assisted pain management tools
Personalized healthcare technologies
Human-centered physiological computing systems

This research also represents an important step toward automated pain assessment in clinical environments where reliable self-reporting may not be available.

title: PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals
text:
Publication
Anup Kumar Gupta, Trishna Saikia, and Puneet Gupta.
PainXtract: A Multimodal System for Multiclass Pain Classification Using Physiological Signals.
Proceedings of the 27th International Conference on Multimodal Interaction (ICMI 2025).
ACM Digital Library: https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3747327.3764792

title: An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging
text:
This work presents an automated framework for oral cancer classification using histopathological images. The proposed approach employs an ensemble learning strategy that combines the strengths of multiple deep learning models to improve classification performance and diagnostic reliability.
The study was published as a book chapter in the proceedings of the International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Communication Technologies (ICICCT 2024).

title: An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging
text:
This work presents an automated framework for oral cancer classification using histopathological images. The proposed approach employs an ensemble learning strategy that combines the strengths of multiple deep learning models to improve classification performance and diagnostic reliability.
The study was published as a book chapter in the proceedings of the International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Communication Technologies (ICICCT 2024).

title: An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging
text:
Motivation
Oral cancer is among the most common forms of cancer worldwide, particularly in developing countries. Early diagnosis plays a crucial role in improving patient survival rates and treatment outcomes.
Histopathological examination remains the gold standard for oral cancer diagnosis. However, manual interpretation of microscopic tissue images is labor-intensive and can be influenced by inter-observer variability. Automated computer-aided diagnostic systems can assist pathologists by providing fast, consistent, and objective analysis.

title: An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging
text:
Method
The proposed framework utilizes an ensemble learning approach for histopathological image classification.

1. Histopathological Image Processing
Microscopic tissue images are preprocessed to improve visual quality and highlight diagnostically relevant structures.


2. Deep Feature Extraction
Multiple deep learning models are employed to learn discriminative representations from histopathological images.


3. Ensemble Classification
Predictions from individual models are combined through an ensemble strategy, allowing the framework to leverage complementary strengths of different architectures and improve overall classification accuracy.

title: An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging
text:
Key Contributions

Developed an automated oral cancer classification system using histopathological images.
Investigated ensemble deep learning techniques for medical image analysis.
Reduced dependence on manual feature engineering.
Improved classification robustness through model aggregation.
Demonstrated the potential of AI-assisted pathology for cancer diagnosis.

title: An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging
text:
Experimental Evaluation
The framework was evaluated on histopathological image datasets for oral cancer classification.
The experimental results demonstrated that:

Ensemble models outperform individual classifiers.
Deep feature representations effectively capture tissue-level characteristics.
Automated systems can support pathologists in cancer diagnosis workflows.

title: An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging
text:
Research Significance
This work contributes to the growing field of computational pathology and AI-assisted cancer diagnosis. By combining histopathological imaging with ensemble learning, the study highlights the potential of deep learning for improving diagnostic accuracy and reducing the workload of medical experts.
The project also strengthened my research experience in medical image analysis and healthcare AI before transitioning toward physiological computing and remote health monitoring.

title: An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging
text:
Publication
Trishna Saikia, Ashutosh Dhamaniya, Puneet Gupta, and Koushlendra Kumar Singh.
An Ensemble Technique for Classification of Oral Cancer by Using Histopathological Imaging.
In: Proceedings of the International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Communication Technologies (ICICCT 2024).
Springer Nature.
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-97-4359-9_66

title: Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos
text:
Pain assessment in neonates remains a challenging clinical problem because infants cannot verbally communicate their discomfort. Existing pain assessment methods often rely on manual observation by healthcare professionals, making them subjective and prone to inter-observer variability.
This work introduces a novel non-contact framework that utilizes remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) extracted from neonatal facial videos for automated pain detection. To the best of our knowledge, this is among the first studies exploring whether physiological signals recovered from facial videos can be used for neonatal pain assessment.

title: Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos
text:
Pain assessment in neonates remains a challenging clinical problem because infants cannot verbally communicate their discomfort. Existing pain assessment methods often rely on manual observation by healthcare professionals, making them subjective and prone to inter-observer variability.
This work introduces a novel non-contact framework that utilizes remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) extracted from neonatal facial videos for automated pain detection. To the best of our knowledge, this is among the first studies exploring whether physiological signals recovered from facial videos can be used for neonatal pain assessment.

title: Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos
text:
Motivation
Unaddressed pain in neonates can lead to adverse short-term and long-term consequences, including developmental delays and impaired growth. Traditional physiological monitoring methods rely on contact-based sensors, which may be uncomfortable for neonates and increase the risk of infection in neonatal intensive care units (NICUs). A non-contact monitoring solution could provide continuous and unobtrusive pain assessment while reducing patient burden.

title: Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos
text:
Method
The proposed framework consists of several stages:

1. Neonatal Face and ROI Detection
Facial regions are localized from neonatal videos, and the forehead region is selected to minimize noise caused by eye blinking and mouth movements. The forehead is further divided into multiple regions of interest (ROIs).


2. Temporal Signal Extraction
RGB temporal signals are extracted from each ROI by averaging pixel intensities across frames. Since neonatal skin exhibits different optical characteristics compared with adults, individual color channels are analyzed separately.


3. Quality-Aware Signal Selection
Not all facial regions provide reliable physiological information. Therefore, a quality metric is used to identify ROIs least affected by skin deformation and motion artifacts. Only the highest-quality temporal signals are retained for further processing.


4. rPPG Signal Estimation
Selected temporal signals are processed using blind source separation techniques to recover pulse-related physiological information. Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is used to identify the cleanest rPPG signal among multiple candidate clips.


5. Pain Classification
The resulting rPPG signal is used for binary pain classification. The study also investigates multimodal fusion by combining physiological information from rPPG signals with audio features extracted from neonatal crying sounds.

title: Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos
text:
Key Contributions

Introduced a novel non-contact neonatal pain detection framework based on rPPG signals.
Demonstrated that physiological signals extracted from facial videos contain useful information for neonatal pain assessment.
Proposed a quality-aware ROI selection strategy for robust rPPG extraction.
Utilized signal-to-noise ratio for identifying the least noisy physiological signals.
Showed that the blue channel provides superior performance for neonatal rPPG estimation compared to red and green channels.
Demonstrated that combining rPPG and audio modalities further improves pain detection performance.

title: Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos
text:
Experimental Findings
Experiments were conducted on the iCOPEvid neonatal pain dataset using subject-independent evaluation protocols.
Key observations include:

Blue-channel rPPG signals consistently outperformed other color channels.
Physiological information extracted from facial videos can effectively discriminate pain and no-pain conditions.
Multimodal fusion of rPPG and audio features achieved better performance than either modality alone.
Signal quality selection played a critical role in improving robustness against motion and facial deformations.

title: Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos
text:
Research Significance
This work extends the scope of remote physiological sensing beyond vital-sign estimation and explores its application in neonatal healthcare. The study demonstrates that subtle physiological changes captured through facial videos can serve as meaningful indicators of pain.
The proposed framework opens new research directions in:

Automated neonatal pain assessment
Non-contact physiological monitoring in NICUs
Multimodal healthcare AI
Explainable physiological computing

It also aligns with the broader goal of developing unobtrusive and patient-friendly healthcare technologies for vulnerable populations.

title: Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos
text:
Publication
Ashutosh Dhamaniya, Anup Kumar Gupta, Trishna Saikia, and Puneet Gupta.
Exploring Remote Photoplethysmography for Neonatal Pain Detection from Facial Videos.
arXiv preprint, 2026.
Available at: https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.25680

title: Classification of Lung Nodules based on Transfer Learning with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
text:
This work presents a lung nodule classification framework that combines deep transfer learning with the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifier for automated analysis of lung CT scan images. The proposed approach leverages feature representations learned by pre-trained deep neural networks and uses KNN as the final classification model to distinguish different categories of lung tumors.
The paper was presented at the 2022 IEEE International Symposium on Technology (IST 2022).

title: Classification of Lung Nodules based on Transfer Learning with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
text:
This work presents a lung nodule classification framework that combines deep transfer learning with the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifier for automated analysis of lung CT scan images. The proposed approach leverages feature representations learned by pre-trained deep neural networks and uses KNN as the final classification model to distinguish different categories of lung tumors.
The paper was presented at the 2022 IEEE International Symposium on Technology (IST 2022).

title: Classification of Lung Nodules based on Transfer Learning with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
text:
Motivation
Lung cancer remains one of the leading causes of cancer-related mortality worldwide. Early identification and classification of lung nodules can significantly improve treatment planning and patient outcomes.
Deep convolutional neural networks have demonstrated strong performance in medical image analysis; however, training such networks from scratch often requires large annotated datasets. Transfer learning provides an effective solution by utilizing knowledge learned from large-scale image datasets and adapting it to medical imaging tasks.

title: Classification of Lung Nodules based on Transfer Learning with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
text:
Method
The proposed framework consists of two stages:

Transfer Learning-based Feature Extraction

A pre-trained convolutional neural network is used to extract discriminative representations from lung CT images.
Transfer learning enables effective feature learning even with limited medical datasets.

K-Nearest Neighbor Classification

Deep features extracted from the network are provided to a KNN classifier.
The classifier assigns lung nodules to their respective categories based on neighborhood similarity in the learned feature space.

title: Classification of Lung Nodules based on Transfer Learning with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
text:
Key Contributions

Developed a hybrid framework combining transfer learning and KNN classification.
Reduced the need for training a deep network from scratch.
Explored deep feature representations for lung nodule categorization.
Demonstrated the effectiveness of combining learned deep features with classical machine learning classifiers.
Contributed to computer-aided diagnosis systems for lung cancer analysis.

title: Classification of Lung Nodules based on Transfer Learning with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
text:
Research Significance
This work represents an important step toward integrating deep learning and traditional machine learning methods for medical image analysis. The study demonstrates how transfer learning can extract meaningful representations from CT images while lightweight classifiers such as KNN can effectively utilize those representations for classification tasks.

title: Classification of Lung Nodules based on Transfer Learning with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
text:
Publication
Trishna Saikia, Malho Hansdah, Koushlendra Kumar Singh, and Manish Kumar Bajpai.
Classification of Lung Nodules based on Transfer Learning with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN).
Proceedings of the 2022 IEEE International Symposium on Technology (IST 2022).
IEEE Xplore: https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9827668

title: An Automatic Lung Nodule Classification System Based on Hybrid Transfer Learning Approach
text:
This work presents an automatic lung nodule classification system based on a hybrid transfer learning approach. The study focuses on classifying lung-patient CT scan images into four categories: small-cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and large-cell carcinoma.
The paper was published in SN Computer Science, Volume 3, Article 272, in 2022.

title: An Automatic Lung Nodule Classification System Based on Hybrid Transfer Learning Approach
text:
This work presents an automatic lung nodule classification system based on a hybrid transfer learning approach. The study focuses on classifying lung-patient CT scan images into four categories: small-cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and large-cell carcinoma.
The paper was published in SN Computer Science, Volume 3, Article 272, in 2022.

title: An Automatic Lung Nodule Classification System Based on Hybrid Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Motivation
Lung cancer is one of the leading causes of cancer-related mortality worldwide. Early and accurate classification of lung nodules from CT images can support clinical decision-making and assist radiologists in identifying different lung cancer subtypes.
Manual analysis of CT scans is time-consuming and may vary across experts. Therefore, automated computer-aided diagnosis systems based on deep learning and transfer learning can provide useful support for medical image analysis.

title: An Automatic Lung Nodule Classification System Based on Hybrid Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Method
The proposed system uses a hybrid transfer learning strategy for lung nodule classification. Transfer learning enables the model to benefit from representations learned by deep convolutional neural networks on large-scale image datasets, which is particularly useful when medical image datasets are limited.
The approach combines feature extraction and classification strategies to improve the representation of CT scan images and classify lung nodules into clinically relevant categories.

title: An Automatic Lung Nodule Classification System Based on Hybrid Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Key Contributions

Developed an automatic lung nodule classification framework using CT scan images.
Applied a hybrid transfer learning strategy for medical image classification.
Classified lung nodules into four lung cancer categories.
Demonstrated the usefulness of deep learning-based computer-aided diagnosis for lung cancer analysis.
Contributed to early research experience in medical imaging and healthcare AI.

title: An Automatic Lung Nodule Classification System Based on Hybrid Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Publication
Trishna Saikia, Ranjeet Kumar, Dilip Kumar, and Koushlendra Kumar Singh. “An Automatic Lung Nodule Classification System Based on Hybrid Transfer Learning Approach.” SN Computer Science, Volume 3, Article 272, 2022.
DOI: 10.1007/s42979-022-01167-0

title: Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach
text:
This work presents a computer-aided framework for automatic plant leaf disease classification using a combination of transfer learning and logistic regression. The proposed system leverages deep feature representations learned from pre-trained convolutional neural networks and employs a lightweight classification model for efficient disease recognition.
The work was published as a book chapter in Machine Vision and Augmented Intelligence, Lecture Notes in Electrical Engineering, Springer, 2023.

title: Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach
text:
This work presents a computer-aided framework for automatic plant leaf disease classification using a combination of transfer learning and logistic regression. The proposed system leverages deep feature representations learned from pre-trained convolutional neural networks and employs a lightweight classification model for efficient disease recognition.
The work was published as a book chapter in Machine Vision and Augmented Intelligence, Lecture Notes in Electrical Engineering, Springer, 2023.

title: Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Motivation
Plant diseases significantly impact agricultural productivity and food security worldwide. Early and accurate identification of leaf diseases can help farmers take timely corrective measures and reduce crop losses.
Traditional manual inspection methods are labor-intensive and often require expert knowledge. Advances in computer vision and deep learning have created opportunities for automated plant disease diagnosis systems that can support precision agriculture and smart farming. Transfer learning is particularly useful in such applications because it allows robust feature extraction even when limited labeled agricultural datasets are available.

title: Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Method
The proposed framework consists of two major stages:

1. Transfer Learning-Based Feature Extraction
A pre-trained deep convolutional neural network is used to extract discriminative visual features from leaf images. Transfer learning enables the model to utilize knowledge learned from large-scale image datasets and adapt it to agricultural disease classification tasks.


2. Logistic Regression Classification
The extracted deep features are provided to a logistic regression classifier that predicts the disease category. Logistic regression offers a simple and computationally efficient alternative while benefiting from the rich feature representations generated by the deep network.

title: Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Key Contributions

Developed an automated framework for plant leaf disease classification.
Combined transfer learning and logistic regression for efficient disease recognition.
Reduced dependence on handcrafted image features.
Demonstrated the usefulness of deep feature representations in agricultural applications.
Contributed to AI-assisted disease monitoring for smart agriculture.

title: Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Experimental Evaluation
The framework was evaluated on plant leaf image datasets containing healthy and diseased leaves. Experimental results demonstrated that transfer learning-based representations significantly improved classification performance compared to conventional feature-engineering approaches.
The study showed that combining deep feature extraction with lightweight classifiers can achieve strong performance while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Research Significance
This work contributes to the growing field of AI for Agriculture by exploring how computer vision and transfer learning can be applied to plant disease diagnosis. Automated disease detection systems can assist farmers in monitoring crop health, reducing yield losses, and improving agricultural sustainability.
The project also strengthened my experience in computer vision, image classification, and transfer learning before transitioning into medical imaging and physiological computing research.

title: Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach
text:
Publication
Trishna Saikia, Malho Hansdah, and Koushlendra Kumar Singh.
Computer-Aided Leaf Disease Classification Based on Logistic Regression and Transfer Learning Approach.
In: Machine Vision and Augmented Intelligence, Lecture Notes in Electrical Engineering, Springer Nature Singapore, 2023.
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-99-0189-0_33

title: Introduction to Various Optimization Techniques
text:
This book chapter provides an introduction to the fundamental concepts of optimization and surveys a variety of optimization techniques used in science, engineering, management, and decision-making problems. The chapter serves as a foundation for understanding how optimization methods can be used to identify the best possible solution among multiple feasible alternatives.
Optimization plays a central role in numerous fields, including engineering design, machine learning, resource allocation, scheduling, logistics, healthcare, and artificial intelligence. The chapter introduces readers to the formulation of optimization problems and the mathematical tools commonly used to solve them.

title: Introduction to Various Optimization Techniques
text:
This book chapter provides an introduction to the fundamental concepts of optimization and surveys a variety of optimization techniques used in science, engineering, management, and decision-making problems. The chapter serves as a foundation for understanding how optimization methods can be used to identify the best possible solution among multiple feasible alternatives.
Optimization plays a central role in numerous fields, including engineering design, machine learning, resource allocation, scheduling, logistics, healthcare, and artificial intelligence. The chapter introduces readers to the formulation of optimization problems and the mathematical tools commonly used to solve them.

title: Introduction to Various Optimization Techniques
text:
Motivation
Many real-world problems require selecting an optimal solution while satisfying various constraints. Examples include:

Minimizing cost in manufacturing systems
Maximizing efficiency in engineering designs
Resource allocation and scheduling
Machine learning model training
Healthcare and biomedical optimization problems

Understanding optimization techniques is therefore essential for researchers and engineers working across disciplines.

title: Introduction to Various Optimization Techniques
text:
Topics Covered
The chapter introduces several important optimization paradigms:

Linear Programming
Optimization of linear objective functions under linear constraints. Linear programming forms the foundation of many resource allocation and operations research problems.


Nonlinear Optimization
Methods for solving problems where objective functions or constraints are nonlinear. These techniques are widely used in engineering design and machine learning applications.


Integer and Combinatorial Optimization
Optimization problems involving discrete decision variables. Such methods are frequently encountered in scheduling, routing, and allocation problems.


Metaheuristic Optimization
Introduction to nature-inspired and population-based optimization methods that can efficiently search large and complex solution spaces. These approaches are useful when exact solutions are computationally expensive.

title: Introduction to Various Optimization Techniques
text:
Key Contributions

Introduced the fundamental principles of optimization.
Discussed formulation of optimization problems including objectives and constraints.
Presented an overview of classical optimization methodologies.
Surveyed modern optimization approaches and their applications.
Provided a foundation for students and researchers entering the field of optimization.

title: Introduction to Various Optimization Techniques
text:
Research Significance
Optimization is one of the mathematical foundations underlying modern machine learning, artificial intelligence, operations research, and engineering design. This chapter was written to provide an accessible introduction to the field and to bridge theoretical concepts with practical engineering applications.
The knowledge presented in this chapter later influenced my research in machine learning, physiological computing, computer vision, and healthcare AI, where optimization plays a critical role in model training and system design.

title: Introduction to Various Optimization Techniques
text:
Book Chapter
Trishna Saikia, Koushlendra Kumar Singh, Sergey Vityazev, and Akbar Sheikh Akbar.
Introduction to Various Optimization Techniques.
Book Chapter published in the IET Digital Library.
Available at: https://digital-library.theiet.org/doi/10.1049/PBPC053E_ch1

title: HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches
text:
HREADAI is a novel remote physiological sensing framework for estimating heart rate from videos of individuals wearing face masks. The work addresses a critical challenge that emerged during and after the COVID-19 pandemic, where traditional rPPG methods relying on full-face visibility experienced significant performance degradation due to facial occlusions.
The framework combines Eulerian and Lagrangian representations of physiological information to robustly estimate heart rate from masked facial videos.
The work was published in IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement (IEEE TIM) in 2023.

title: HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches
text:
HREADAI is a novel remote physiological sensing framework for estimating heart rate from videos of individuals wearing face masks. The work addresses a critical challenge that emerged during and after the COVID-19 pandemic, where traditional rPPG methods relying on full-face visibility experienced significant performance degradation due to facial occlusions.
The framework combines Eulerian and Lagrangian representations of physiological information to robustly estimate heart rate from masked facial videos.
The work was published in IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement (IEEE TIM) in 2023.

title: HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches
text:
Motivation
Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) enables contactless heart rate monitoring by analyzing subtle physiological changes captured in facial videos. However, most existing approaches assume that large facial regions are visible and therefore struggle when subjects wear masks.
To overcome this limitation, HREADAI investigates whether physiological information can still be reliably recovered from partially visible facial regions by jointly exploiting:

Color-based physiological variations (Eulerian information)
Motion-based physiological variations (Lagrangian information)

The study demonstrates that combining these complementary cues leads to more robust heart rate estimation under facial occlusion.

title: HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches
text:
Method
The proposed framework consists of four major stages:

1. Facial Region Localization
Facial landmarks are detected using MediaPipe to identify visible facial regions despite mask-induced occlusion.


2. Eulerian Physiological Signal Extraction
The Eulerian branch extracts subtle color variations caused by blood volume changes. These variations contain cardiac information that can be transformed into pulse signals.


3. Lagrangian Physiological Signal Extraction
The Lagrangian branch tracks minute facial motions associated with cardiovascular activity using feature-point motion trajectories.


4. Quality-Aware Signal Fusion
Signals obtained from both branches are evaluated using quality measures and combined to generate a robust heart rate estimate. The fusion mechanism allows the framework to exploit complementary physiological information under varying recording conditions.

title: HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches
text:
Key Contributions

Proposed one of the first heart rate estimation frameworks specifically designed for masked-face videos.
Introduced a hybrid Eulerian-Lagrangian physiological sensing framework.
Demonstrated that motion-based physiological information can complement color-based rPPG signals.
Developed a quality-aware fusion strategy for reliable heart rate estimation.
Improved robustness against facial occlusions introduced by masks.
Established an effective framework for contactless physiological monitoring in real-world conditions.

title: HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches
text:
Experimental Evaluation
The framework was evaluated on facial videos recorded under mask-wearing conditions and compared against state-of-the-art heart rate estimation approaches.
Experimental results demonstrated that:

Eulerian and Lagrangian signals provide complementary physiological information.
Fusion significantly improves estimation accuracy compared to individual modalities.
Reliable heart rate estimation remains possible even when large facial regions are occluded by masks.

title: HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches
text:
Research Significance
HREADAI represents one of the earliest efforts to systematically investigate physiological sensing from masked facial videos. The work demonstrates that combining multiple physiological representations can substantially improve robustness and generalization in remote health monitoring systems.
The ideas introduced in HREADAI later influenced subsequent research directions in respiratory rate estimation, blood pressure estimation, SpO₂ estimation, and multimodal physiological computing.

title: HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches
text:
Publication
Trishna Saikia, Lokendra Birla, Anup Kumar Gupta, and Puneet Gupta.
HREADAI: Heart Rate Estimation From Face Mask Videos by Consolidating Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches.
IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement (TIM), 2023.
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1109/TIM.2023.3336118

title: HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks
text:
HR-TRACK is a deep learning-based framework for contactless heart rate monitoring from facial videos using remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) and Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs). The method is designed to robustly estimate heart rate under real-world conditions while effectively modeling temporal dependencies present in physiological signals.
The work was presented at the Pattern Recognition and Machine Intelligence (PReMI 2024) conference and published in the Lecture Notes in Computer Science (LNCS) series by Springer.

title: HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks
text:
HR-TRACK is a deep learning-based framework for contactless heart rate monitoring from facial videos using remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) and Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs). The method is designed to robustly estimate heart rate under real-world conditions while effectively modeling temporal dependencies present in physiological signals.
The work was presented at the Pattern Recognition and Machine Intelligence (PReMI 2024) conference and published in the Lecture Notes in Computer Science (LNCS) series by Springer.

title: HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks
text:
Motivation
Remote photoplethysmography enables non-contact estimation of heart rate by analyzing subtle skin color variations caused by blood volume changes. Although several conventional signal-processing and deep-learning approaches have been proposed, accurately modeling long-range temporal dependencies remains challenging, particularly in the presence of motion, illumination changes, and facial expression variations.
HR-TRACK was developed to address these challenges by utilizing Temporal Convolutional Networks, which are well suited for capturing temporal patterns while maintaining efficient training and inference.

title: HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks
text:
Method
The proposed framework consists of the following stages:

1. Facial Video Processing
Facial regions are detected and tracked across video frames to obtain stable temporal observations containing physiological information.


2. rPPG Signal Representation
Remote physiological information is extracted from facial videos by analyzing subtle color variations associated with cardiovascular activity.


3. Temporal Convolutional Network
A Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) is employed to model temporal relationships within the extracted physiological signals. Compared with recurrent architectures, TCNs offer:

Larger effective receptive fields
Stable gradient propagation
Efficient parallel computation
Improved long-range temporal modeling



4. Heart Rate Estimation
The learned temporal representations are mapped to heart rate predictions, enabling robust physiological monitoring from facial videos.

title: HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks
text:
Key Contributions

Proposed a Temporal Convolutional Network-based framework for rPPG-driven heart rate estimation.
Investigated deep temporal modeling for physiological signal analysis.
Demonstrated the effectiveness of TCNs in capturing long-term cardiac dynamics.
Improved robustness against variations in video quality and environmental conditions.
Advanced the integration of deep learning and physiological computing for contactless health monitoring.

title: HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks
text:
Experimental Evaluation
The framework was evaluated on publicly available rPPG datasets for heart rate estimation.
Experimental results demonstrated that:

Temporal convolutional learning effectively captures physiological signal dynamics.
TCN-based modeling improves heart rate estimation performance compared with traditional signal-processing approaches.
The framework generalizes well across different recording conditions and subjects.

title: HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks
text:
Research Significance
HR-TRACK represents an important step in the evolution of deep learning approaches for remote physiological sensing. The work explores how temporal convolutional architectures can be leveraged for physiological signal understanding and provides insights into robust heart rate estimation from videos.
This research also laid the foundation for several subsequent projects in our group involving:

Blood pressure estimation from rPPG
Respiratory rate estimation
SpO₂ estimation
Physiological signal quality assessment
Multimodal physiological computing

The ideas explored in HR-TRACK contributed to the broader research direction of developing reliable, real-world healthcare AI systems based on non-contact physiological sensing.

title: HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks
text:
Publication
Lokendra Birla, Sneha Shukla Trishna Saikia, and Puneet Gupta.
HR-TRACK: An rPPG Method for Heartrate Monitoring Using Temporal Convolution Networks.
In: Pattern Recognition and Machine Intelligence (PReMI 2024). Lecture Notes in Computer Science (LNCS), Springer.
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-78201-5_24

title: AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information
text:
AVENUE is a novel deepfake detection framework that exploits physiological information embedded in facial videos. Unlike appearance-based methods that focus on visual artifacts, AVENUE utilizes remote Photoplethysmography (rPPG) signals and temporal modeling to identify inconsistencies introduced during deepfake generation.
The work was published in ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (ACM TIST) in 2025.

title: AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information
text:
AVENUE is a novel deepfake detection framework that exploits physiological information embedded in facial videos. Unlike appearance-based methods that focus on visual artifacts, AVENUE utilizes remote Photoplethysmography (rPPG) signals and temporal modeling to identify inconsistencies introduced during deepfake generation.
The work was published in ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (ACM TIST) in 2025.

title: AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information
text:
Motivation
Recent deepfake generation methods based on GANs and diffusion models can produce highly realistic facial videos, making traditional appearance-based detection increasingly challenging.
However, synthetic videos often fail to accurately reproduce subtle physiological patterns such as blood-volume-induced skin color changes. These physiological signals can be recovered through remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) and serve as a reliable cue for distinguishing authentic videos from manipulated ones. AVENUE was developed to exploit this observation while addressing the challenges of motion, facial deformation, and illumination-induced noise.

title: AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information
text:
Method
The AVENUE framework consists of three key components:

1. Stable Clip Selection
Facial motion and deformation introduce significant noise into rPPG signals. AVENUE identifies and selects relatively stable facial clips that are least affected by motion artifacts, resulting in more reliable physiological information extraction.


2. rPPG-Based Physiological Representation
The selected clips are processed to extract rPPG signals that capture subtle blood-volume changes from facial skin regions. Since deepfake generators generally do not model physiological consistency, these signals provide discriminative information for detection.


3. Modified Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN)
Instead of recurrent architectures, AVENUE employs a modified Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) capable of modeling long-range temporal dependencies efficiently. This allows the framework to capture temporal inconsistencies that arise in manipulated videos.

title: AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information
text:
Key Contributions

Proposed a novel rPPG-based deepfake detection framework.
Introduced stable clip selection to improve physiological signal quality.
Designed a modified Temporal Convolutional Network for long-sequence modeling.
Demonstrated that physiological cues provide robust information against advanced facial manipulation techniques.
Achieved superior performance compared with existing rPPG-based deepfake detection methods on publicly available benchmarks.

title: AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information
text:
Datasets and Evaluation
The framework was evaluated on publicly available deepfake datasets including:

FaceForensics++
Celeb-DF
DFDC

Experimental results showed improved robustness and generalization compared to existing physiological-signal-based deepfake detection approaches.

title: AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information
text:
Research Significance
AVENUE demonstrates that physiological information remains a powerful cue for detecting manipulated media even as visual quality improves. The work contributes to the growing field of trustworthy AI and multimedia forensics by combining computer vision, physiological computing, and temporal deep learning.
The research also established a foundation for subsequent work exploring physiological signal analysis beyond healthcare applications, extending rPPG into the domain of media authenticity verification.

title: AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information
text:
Publication
Lokendra Birla, Trishna Saikia, and Puneet Gupta.
AVENUE: A Novel Deepfake Detection Method Based on Temporal Convolutional Network and rPPG Information.
ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (TIST), 2025.
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1145/3702232
